title: Sent. n. sez. 1337
text:
The case involves O.L., a male accused of crimes related to drug trafficking, and O.M. , a Nigerian woman recruited at the age of 18 by a Nigerian criminal organization to work as a babysitter in Italy. She endured a harrowing journey involving confinement and physical and sexual violence, including in Libya. Upon her arrival in Italy, she was forced into prostitution to repay her travel debt under the threat of harm to her grandmother in Nigeria. To escape this violence, she accepted a proposal from O.L. to work as a drug courier driven by urgent financial need.

On January 9, 2023, the Court of Appeal of Rome confirmed the ruling of the Court of Rome that convicted O.L. for drug-related crimes. In the case of O.M. , she was acquitted of drug trafficking conspiracy but was convicted of transporting narcotics.

O.L. and O.M. appealed to the Supreme Court. On November 16, 2023, the Supreme Court declared O.L.'s appeal inadmissible. In O.M. 's case, the Supreme Court annulled the previous judgment and referred the matter to a different section of the Rome Court of Appeal for a retrial. The Supreme Court found that the original judgment had failed to recognize the appellant's socio-economic vulnerability and had erroneously suggested, without substantial evidence, that O.M. could seek help from public institutions and escape the power of her compatriots. Moreover, the judgment neglected to consider the severe danger posed to O.M. and her family in Nigeria if the debt remained unpaid—factors crucial for establishing grounds for non-punishment under Article 54 of the Italian Penal Code.

According to the Supreme Court, the assessment that the new trial judge is required to make to determine whether the prerequisites of Article 54 of the Penal Code exist regarding the commission of the drug-related crime by the defendant, as a trafficking victim, must concern, among other things: her personal conditions when she left Nigeria and the experiences during the journey, including in transit countries (declared or identified); her living situation in Italy at the time of the crime (with specific reference to the work context, relationship with compatriots, sources of livelihood, and sexual exploitation suffered); the existence of a debt, its amount, and the reasons it was incurred; the necessity to repay it with specific concern for her family; the peculiar condition of subjugation to another's will; the conviction of not being able to escape the control of her exploiter and not being able to turn to the authorities; the link between the nature of the imminent danger to the appellant and the crime for which she is being tried; the risks she would have faced if she had refused, with reference to possible, even indirect, retaliations by her exploiters.
